The term "moor" derives from the German word "Moor," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in alluvial plains often pass through peat layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in {{ill|Otofuke|ja|音更町}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|Tokachigawa Onsen, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|Sub-bituminous coal]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid, giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "Moor," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in alluvial plains often pass through peat layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in {{ill|Otofuke|ja|音更町}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Black Water|ja|黒湯}}

The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉}}, depending on their specific components.

== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|Tokachigawa Onsen, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|Sub-bituminous coal]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid, giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>

== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|Tokachigawa Onsen, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|Sub-bituminous coal]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid, giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "Moor," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in alluvial plains often pass through peat layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in {{ill|Otofuke|ja|音更町}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|Tokachigawa Onsen, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|Sub-bituminous coal]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid, giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "Moor," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in alluvial plains often pass through peat layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in {{ill|Otofuke|ja|音更町}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Black Water|ja|黒湯}}

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|Tokachigawa Onsen, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|Sub-bituminous coal]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid, giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "Moor," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in alluvial plains often pass through peat layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in {{ill|Otofuke|ja|音更町}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />

Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in {{ill|Otofuke|ja|音更町}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|Tokachigawa Onsen, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|Sub-bituminous coal]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid, giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "Moor," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in alluvial plains often pass through peat layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in {{ill|Otofuke|ja|音更町}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|Tokachigawa Onsen, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|Sub-bituminous coal]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid, giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "Moor," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in alluvial plains often pass through peat layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Black Water|ja|黒湯}}

These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|Tokachigawa Onsen, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|Sub-bituminous coal]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid, giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "Moor," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in alluvial plains often pass through peat layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />

== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "Moor," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in alluvial plains often pass through peat layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|Tokachigawa Onsen, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|Sub-bituminous coal]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid, giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "Moor," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in alluvial plains often pass through peat layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉|1=Chloride spring}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|Tokachigawa Onsen, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|Sub-bituminous coal]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid, giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "Moor," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in alluvial plains often pass through peat layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉|1=Chloride spring}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Black Water|ja|黒湯}}

revision.text:
[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|Tokachigawa Onsen, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|Sub-bituminous coal]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid, giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "Moor," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in alluvial plains often pass through peat layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉|1=Chloride spring}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Black Water|ja|黒湯|1=Kuroyu}}

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|Tokachigawa Onsen, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|Sub-bituminous coal]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid, giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "Moor," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in alluvial plains often pass through peat layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉|1=Chloride spring}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|Tokachigawa Onsen, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|Sub-bituminous coal]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid, giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "Moor," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in alluvial plains often pass through peat layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉|1=Brine spring}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Black Water|ja|黒湯|1=Kuroyu}}

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|Tokachigawa Onsen, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|Sub-bituminous coal]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid, giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref><ref>[https://www.aprotraveljapan.com/post/hokkaido-heritage-moor-onsen Hokkaido Heritage 'Moor Onsen']</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "Moor," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in alluvial plains often pass through peat layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉|1=Brine spring}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}}, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Sub-bituminous_coal|Sub-bituminous coal]]]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing [[:en:Humic_substance|humic substances]] like humic acid and [[:en:Fulvic_acid|fulvic acid]], giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref><ref name=":0">[https://www.aprotraveljapan.com/post/hokkaido-heritage-moor-onsen Hokkaido Heritage 'Moor Onsen']</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:de:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉|1=Brine spring}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture
