== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}}, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Sub-bituminous_coal|Sub-bituminous coal]]]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing [[:en:Humic_substance|humic substances]] like humic acid and [[:en:Fulvic_acid|fulvic acid]], giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref><ref name=":0">[https://www.aprotraveljapan.com/post/hokkaido-heritage-moor-onsen Hokkaido Heritage 'Moor Onsen']</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:de:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉|1=Brine spring}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉|1=Brine spring}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}}, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Sub-bituminous_coal|Sub-bituminous coal]]]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing [[:en:Humic_substance|humic substances]] like humic acid and [[:en:Fulvic_acid|fulvic acid]], giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref><ref name=":0">[https://www.aprotraveljapan.com/post/hokkaido-heritage-moor-onsen Hokkaido Heritage 'Moor Onsen']</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:de:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉|1=Brine spring}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

revision.text:
[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}}, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Sub-bituminous_coal|Sub-bituminous coal]]]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing [[:en:Humic_substance|humic substances]] like humic acid and [[:en:Fulvic_acid|fulvic acid]], giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref><ref name=":0">[https://www.aprotraveljapan.com/post/hokkaido-heritage-moor-onsen Hokkaido Heritage 'Moor Onsen']</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:de:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉|1=Brine spring}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Black Water|ja|黒湯|1=Kuroyu}}

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}}, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Sub-bituminous_coal|Sub-bituminous coal]]]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing [[:en:Humic_substance|humic substances]] like humic acid and [[:en:Fulvic_acid|fulvic acid]], giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref><ref name=":0">[https://www.aprotraveljapan.com/post/hokkaido-heritage-moor-onsen Hokkaido Heritage 'Moor Onsen']</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:de:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉|1=Brine spring}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|12=draft}}, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Sub-bituminous_coal|Sub-bituminous coal]]]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing [[:en:Humic_substance|humic substances]] like humic acid and [[:en:Fulvic_acid|fulvic acid]], giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref><ref name=":0">[https://www.aprotraveljapan.com/post/hokkaido-heritage-moor-onsen Hokkaido Heritage 'Moor Onsen']</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:de:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|12=draft}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道|12=draft}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|12=draft}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|12=draft}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省|12=draft}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉|1=Brine spring|12=draft}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|12=draft}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア|12=draft}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン|12=draft}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯|12=draft}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|12=draft}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産|12=draft}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市|12=draft}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture
== References ==
<references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Black Water|ja|黒湯|1=Kuroyu|12=draft}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:1 revision imported]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|12=draft}}, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Sub-bituminous_coal|Sub-bituminous coal]]]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing [[:en:Humic_substance|humic substances]] like humic acid and [[:en:Fulvic_acid|fulvic acid]], giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref><ref name=":0">[https://www.aprotraveljapan.com/post/hokkaido-heritage-moor-onsen Hokkaido Heritage 'Moor Onsen']</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:de:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|12=draft}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道|12=draft}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|12=draft}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|12=draft}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省|12=draft}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉|1=Brine spring|12=draft}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|12=draft}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア|12=draft}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン|12=draft}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯|12=draft}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|12=draft}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産|12=draft}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市|12=draft}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture
== References ==
<references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Black Water|ja|黒湯|1=Kuroyu|12=draft}}

revision.text:
[[ファイル:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|十勝川温泉のモール温泉]] [[ファイル:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|亜炭]] '''モール泉'''（モールせん）とは、[[泥炭]]層を通して湧出する[[フミン酸]]や[[フルボ酸]]といった透明で褐色を帯びた腐植物質を含む温泉<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=井上 源喜 |author2=清水 理紗子 |author3= 名倉 美佳 |title=東京都大田区地域における黒湯の地球化学的特徴 |journal=日本地球化学会年会要旨集 |volume=66 |issue= |publisher=一般社団法人日本地球化学会 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=「まち、ひと、北海道遺産第2章」北海道遺産情報誌 vol.6|publisher=北海道遺産構想推進協議会 |date= |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>。'''モール温泉'''または'''モール系温泉'''<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=高野 敬志 |author2=内野 栄治 |author3=青柳 直樹 |title=北海道におけるモール系温泉の腐植物質成分と外観指標 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=68 |issue= |publisher= |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>ともいう。
== 概要 == モールは[[ドイツ語]]で泥炭を意味するMoorに由来している<ref name="isan" />。堆積平野などから湧出する非火山性の温泉には、泥炭層などを通過して湧出することで鉱物成分よりも植物成分が多く、フミン酸やフルボ酸などの腐植物質によって褐色の湯が湧き出るものがあり、これをモール泉（モール温泉、モール系温泉）という<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />。
モール泉が湧き出る代表的な場所として、[[ドイツ連邦共和国]]の[[バーデン＝バーデン]]があり、[[日本]]では[[北海道]][[音更町]]の[[十勝川温泉]]や[[石狩平野]]、[[豊富町]]などが知られている<ref name="isan" />。
溶存有機物（DOM）であるフミン酸やフルボ酸で構成される腐植物質によって褐色に着色した温泉を「褐色温泉」という<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=高野 敬志 |author2=内野 栄治 |author3= 青柳 直樹 |title=北海道内で湧出する褐色温泉に含まれる溶存有機物質及び腐植物質中の炭素、窒素及びリン含量 |journal=陸水学雑誌 |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=日本陸水學會 |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>。褐色温泉には「モール系温泉」（モール泉）あるいは「[[黒湯]]」と呼ばれるものがあり、地域によって、北海道などでは「モール系温泉」、[[関東地方]]などでは「黒湯」と呼ばれている<ref name="takano2018" />。ただ名称だけでなく成分についても、神奈川県横浜・川崎地域の黒湯と北海道のモール系温泉を比較すると、黒湯のほうが[[C/N比]]が低く[[C/P比]]が高いという報告がある<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=高野 敬志 |author2=井上 源喜 |author3= 内野 栄治 |title=炭素/窒素比および炭素/リン比からみた神奈川県横浜・川崎地域に湧出する黒湯に含まれる腐植物質の特性 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=70 |issue=3 |publisher= |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>。
日本の[[温泉法]]の[[温泉#療養泉|療養泉]]の定義には腐植物質に関係する項目がなく、これに対応する区分は設けられていないが、[[環境省]]の鉱泉分析法指針には「フミン酸（腐植質）」の項目がある<ref name="takano2019" />。なお、療養泉の分類上では[[単純温泉]]や[[塩化物泉]]、[[炭酸水素塩泉]]などとなっており、[[効能]]などはそれぞれに準じる。
なお、[[北欧諸国]]や[[ロシア]]では泥炭を微粉末にして浴用とすることがある<ref name="isan" />。
== 代表的なモール泉 == * [[バーデン＝バーデン]]<ref name="isan" /> * [[西那須野温泉#杜の湯|西那須野温泉(杜の湯)]] * [[十勝川温泉]] - [[北海道遺産]]に選定されている<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html モール温泉]NPO法人北海道遺産協議会事務局</ref>。 * [[愛媛県]][[八幡浜市]]<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html 八幡浜黒湯温泉みなと湯]</ref>。中国・四国地方初のモール泉。 * りんご温泉 - 山形県西村山郡朝日町
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
== 関連項目 == * [[黒湯]]
{{温泉}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:もおるせん}} [[Category:温泉]] [[Category:北海道遺産]]

[[ファイル:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|十勝川温泉のモール温泉]] [[ファイル:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|亜炭]] '''モール泉'''（モールせん）とは、[[泥炭]]層を通して湧出する[[フミン酸]]や[[フルボ酸]]といった透明で褐色を帯びた腐植物質を含む温泉<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=井上 源喜 |author2=清水 理紗子 |author3= 名倉 美佳 |title=東京都大田区地域における黒湯の地球化学的特徴 |journal=日本地球化学会年会要旨集 |volume=66 |issue= |publisher=一般社団法人日本地球化学会 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=「まち、ひと、北海道遺産第2章」北海道遺産情報誌 vol.6|publisher=北海道遺産構想推進協議会 |date= |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>。'''モール温泉'''または'''モール系温泉'''<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=高野 敬志 |author2=内野 栄治 |author3=青柳 直樹 |title=北海道におけるモール系温泉の腐植物質成分と外観指標 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=68 |issue= |publisher= |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>ともいう。
== 概要 == モールは[[ドイツ語]]で泥炭を意味するMoorに由来している<ref name="isan" />。堆積平野などから湧出する非火山性の温泉には、泥炭層などを通過して湧出することで鉱物成分よりも植物成分が多く、フミン酸やフルボ酸などの腐植物質によって褐色の湯が湧き出るものがあり、これをモール泉（モール温泉、モール系温泉）という<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />。
モール泉が湧き出る代表的な場所として、[[ドイツ連邦共和国]]の[[バーデン＝バーデン]]があり、[[日本]]では[[北海道]][[音更町]]の[[十勝川温泉]]や[[石狩平野]]、[[豊富町]]などが知られている<ref name="isan" />。
溶存有機物（DOM）であるフミン酸やフルボ酸で構成される腐植物質によって褐色に着色した温泉を「褐色温泉」という<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=高野 敬志 |author2=内野 栄治 |author3= 青柳 直樹 |title=北海道内で湧出する褐色温泉に含まれる溶存有機物質及び腐植物質中の炭素、窒素及びリン含量 |journal=陸水学雑誌 |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=日本陸水學會 |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>。褐色温泉には「モール系温泉」（モール泉）あるいは「[[黒湯]]」と呼ばれるものがあり、地域によって、北海道などでは「モール系温泉」、[[関東地方]]などでは「黒湯」と呼ばれている<ref name="takano2018" />。ただ名称だけでなく成分についても、神奈川県横浜・川崎地域の黒湯と北海道のモール系温泉を比較すると、黒湯のほうが[[C/N比]]が低く[[C/P比]]が高いという報告がある<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=高野 敬志 |author2=井上 源喜 |author3= 内野 栄治 |title=炭素/窒素比および炭素/リン比からみた神奈川県横浜・川崎地域に湧出する黒湯に含まれる腐植物質の特性 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=70 |issue=3 |publisher= |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>。
日本の[[温泉法]]の[[温泉#療養泉|療養泉]]の定義には腐植物質に関係する項目がなく、これに対応する区分は設けられていないが、[[環境省]]の鉱泉分析法指針には「フミン酸（腐植質）」の項目がある<ref name="takano2019" />。なお、療養泉の分類上では[[単純温泉]]や[[塩化物泉]]、[[炭酸水素塩泉]]などとなっており、[[効能]]などはそれぞれに準じる。
なお、[[北欧諸国]]や[[ロシア]]では泥炭を微粉末にして浴用とすることがある<ref name="isan" />。
== 代表的なモール泉 == * [[バーデン＝バーデン]]<ref name="isan" /> * [[西那須野温泉#杜の湯|西那須野温泉(杜の湯)]] * [[十勝川温泉]] - [[北海道遺産]]に選定されている<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html モール温泉]NPO法人北海道遺産協議会事務局</ref>。 * [[愛媛県]][[八幡浜市]]<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html 八幡浜黒湯温泉みなと湯]</ref>。中国・四国地方初のモール泉。 * りんご温泉 - 山形県西村山郡朝日町

== 関連項目 == * [[黒湯]]

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|qq=draft}}, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Sub-bituminous_coal|Sub-bituminous coal]]]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing [[:en:Humic_substance|humic substances]] like humic acid and [[:en:Fulvic_acid|fulvic acid]], giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref><ref name=":0">[https://www.aprotraveljapan.com/post/hokkaido-heritage-moor-onsen Hokkaido Heritage 'Moor Onsen']</ref><ref>https://www.hokkaidohotel.co.jp/english/stay/hotspring.html</ref><ref>https://www.daiichihotel.com/eng/spa/</ref><ref>https://att-japan.net/en/446-2/</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:de:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /><ref>http://www.tokachigawa.net/english/destination/tokachigawa.html</ref> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|qq=draft}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道|qq=draft}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|qq=draft}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省|1=Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|lt=Ministry of the Environment|qq=draft}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉|1=Brine spring|1=Brine spring|qq=draft}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア|qq=draft}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン|qq=draft}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|qq=draft}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産|qq=draft}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市|qq=draft}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture
== References ==
<references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Black Water|ja|黒湯|1=Kuroyu|qq=draft}}

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|qq=draft}}, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Sub-bituminous_coal|Sub-bituminous coal]]]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing [[:en:Humic_substance|humic substances]] like humic acid and [[:en:Fulvic_acid|fulvic acid]], giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref><ref name=":0">[https://www.aprotraveljapan.com/post/hokkaido-heritage-moor-onsen Hokkaido Heritage 'Moor Onsen']</ref><ref>https://www.hokkaidohotel.co.jp/english/stay/hotspring.html</ref><ref>https://www.daiichihotel.com/eng/spa/</ref><ref>https://att-japan.net/en/446-2/</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>

The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|qq=draft}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省|1=Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|lt=Ministry of the Environment|qq=draft}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉|1=Brine spring|1=Brine spring|qq=draft}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア|qq=draft}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン|qq=draft}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|qq=draft}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産|qq=draft}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市|qq=draft}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|qq=draft}}, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Sub-bituminous_coal|Sub-bituminous coal]]]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing [[:en:Humic_substance|humic substances]] like humic acid and [[:en:Fulvic_acid|fulvic acid]], giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref><ref name=":0">[https://www.aprotraveljapan.com/post/hokkaido-heritage-moor-onsen Hokkaido Heritage 'Moor Onsen']</ref><ref>https://www.hokkaidohotel.co.jp/english/stay/hotspring.html</ref><ref>https://www.daiichihotel.com/eng/spa/</ref><ref>https://att-japan.net/en/446-2/</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:de:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /><ref>http://www.tokachigawa.net/english/destination/tokachigawa.html</ref> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|qq=draft}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道|qq=draft}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|qq=draft}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省|1=Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|lt=Ministry of the Environment|qq=draft}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉|1=Brine spring|1=Brine spring|qq=draft}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア|qq=draft}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン|qq=draft}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|qq=draft}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産|qq=draft}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市|qq=draft}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Germany|qq=}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokkaido|qq=}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō Region|qq=}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省|1=Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|lt=Ministry of the Environment|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Environmental Ministry|13=User:Immanuelle/Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|qq=}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉|1=Brine spring|1=Brine spring|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chloride Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Brine spring|qq=}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Russia|qq=}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baden-Baden|qq=}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|qq=}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokkaido Heritage|qq=}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawatahama|qq=}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}}, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Sub-bituminous_coal|Sub-bituminous coal]]]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing [[:en:Humic_substance|humic substances]] like humic acid and [[:en:Fulvic_acid|fulvic acid]], giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref><ref name=":0">[https://www.aprotraveljapan.com/post/hokkaido-heritage-moor-onsen Hokkaido Heritage 'Moor Onsen']</ref><ref>https://www.hokkaidohotel.co.jp/english/stay/hotspring.html</ref><ref>https://www.daiichihotel.com/eng/spa/</ref><ref>https://att-japan.net/en/446-2/</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:de:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /><ref>http://www.tokachigawa.net/english/destination/tokachigawa.html</ref> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Germany|qq=}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokkaido|qq=}}, as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in {{ill|Kantō Region|ja|関東地方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō Region|qq=}}.<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the {{ill|Environmental Ministry|ja|環境省|1=Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|lt=Ministry of the Environment|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Environmental Ministry|13=User:Immanuelle/Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|qq=}} guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, {{ill|Chloride Springs|ja|塩化物泉|1=Brine spring|1=Brine spring|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chloride Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Brine spring|qq=}}, or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and {{ill|Russia|ja|ロシア|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Russia|qq=}}, finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * {{ill|Baden-Baden|ja|バーデン＝バーデン|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baden-Baden|qq=}}<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|qq=}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}} - Designated as a {{ill|Hokkaido Heritage|ja|北海道遺産|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokkaido Heritage|qq=}}.<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * {{ill|Yawatahama|ja|八幡浜市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawatahama|qq=}}, Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:de:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /><ref>http://www.tokachigawa.net/english/destination/tokachigawa.html</ref> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Germany|qq=}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō Region]].<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, [[:en:Brine spring|Brine spring]], or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and [[:en:Russia|Russia]], finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * [[:en:Baden-Baden|Baden-Baden]]<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|qq=}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}} - Designated as a [[:en:Hokkaido Heritage|Hokkaido Heritage]].<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * [[:en:Yawatahama|Yawatahama]], Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, [[:en:Brine spring|Brine spring]], or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and [[:en:Russia|Russia]], finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * [[:en:Baden-Baden|Baden-Baden]]<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|qq=}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}} - Designated as a [[:en:Hokkaido Heritage|Hokkaido Heritage]].<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * [[:en:Yawatahama|Yawatahama]], Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

<!--Moor hot springs--><!--Moor hot springs--><!--Moor hot springs--><!--Moor hot springs--><!--Moor hot springs--><!--Moor hot springs--><!--Moor hot springs--><!--Moor hot springs--><!--Moor hot springs--> [[Category:Hokkaido Heritage]]

[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}}, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Sub-bituminous_coal|Sub-bituminous coal]]]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing [[:en:Humic_substance|humic substances]] like humic acid and [[:en:Fulvic_acid|fulvic acid]], giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref><ref name=":0">[https://www.aprotraveljapan.com/post/hokkaido-heritage-moor-onsen Hokkaido Heritage 'Moor Onsen']</ref><ref>https://www.hokkaidohotel.co.jp/english/stay/hotspring.html</ref><ref>https://www.daiichihotel.com/eng/spa/</ref><ref>https://att-japan.net/en/446-2/</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:de:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /><ref>http://www.tokachigawa.net/english/destination/tokachigawa.html</ref> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Germany|qq=}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō Region]].<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, [[:en:Brine spring|Brine spring]], or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and [[:en:Russia|Russia]], finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * [[:en:Baden-Baden|Baden-Baden]]<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|qq=}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}} - Designated as a [[:en:Hokkaido Heritage|Hokkaido Heritage]].<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * [[:en:Yawatahama|Yawatahama]], Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture
