[[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}}, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Sub-bituminous_coal|Sub-bituminous coal]]]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing [[:en:Humic_substance|humic substances]] like humic acid and [[:en:Fulvic_acid|fulvic acid]], giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref><ref name=":0">[https://www.aprotraveljapan.com/post/hokkaido-heritage-moor-onsen Hokkaido Heritage 'Moor Onsen']</ref><ref>https://www.hokkaidohotel.co.jp/english/stay/hotspring.html</ref><ref>https://www.daiichihotel.com/eng/spa/</ref><ref>https://att-japan.net/en/446-2/</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:de:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /><ref>http://www.tokachigawa.net/english/destination/tokachigawa.html</ref> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Germany|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ドイツ}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō Region]].<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, [[:en:Brine spring|Brine spring]], or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and [[:en:Russia|Russia]], finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * [[:en:Baden-Baden|Baden-Baden]]<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|qq=}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}} - Designated as a [[:en:Hokkaido Heritage|Hokkaido Heritage]].<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * [[:en:Yawatahama|Yawatahama]], Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Germany|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ドイツ}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō Region]].<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, [[:en:Brine spring|Brine spring]], or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and [[:en:Russia|Russia]], finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * [[:en:Baden-Baden|Baden-Baden]]<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|qq=}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}} - Designated as a [[:en:Hokkaido Heritage|Hokkaido Heritage]].<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * [[:en:Yawatahama|Yawatahama]], Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kuroyu|ja|黒湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroyu|qq=}}

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11344950]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}}, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Sub-bituminous_coal|Sub-bituminous coal]]]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing [[:en:Humic_substance|humic substances]] like humic acid and [[:en:Fulvic_acid|fulvic acid]], giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref><ref name=":0">[https://www.aprotraveljapan.com/post/hokkaido-heritage-moor-onsen Hokkaido Heritage 'Moor Onsen']</ref><ref>https://www.hokkaidohotel.co.jp/english/stay/hotspring.html</ref><ref>https://www.daiichihotel.com/eng/spa/</ref><ref>https://att-japan.net/en/446-2/</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:de:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /><ref>http://www.tokachigawa.net/english/destination/tokachigawa.html</ref> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Germany|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ドイツ}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō Region]].<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, [[:en:Brine spring|Brine spring]], or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and [[:en:Russia|Russia]], finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * [[:en:Baden-Baden|Baden-Baden]]<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|qq=}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}} - Designated as a [[:en:Hokkaido Heritage|Hokkaido Heritage]].<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * [[:en:Yawatahama|Yawatahama]], Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kuroyu|ja|黒湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroyu|qq=}}

The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, [[:en:Brine spring|Brine spring]], or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.

<!--Moor hot springs--><!--Moor hot springs--><!--Moor hot springs--><!--Moor hot springs--><!--Moor hot springs--><!--Moor hot springs--><!--Moor hot springs--><!--Moor hot springs--><!--Moor hot springs--> [[Category:Hokkaido Heritage]] [[Category:Hot_springs_of_Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[Category:Hot Spring Types]] {{wikidata link|Q11344950}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:モール泉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11344950]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}}, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Sub-bituminous_coal|Sub-bituminous coal]]]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing [[:en:Humic_substance|humic substances]] like humic acid and [[:en:Fulvic_acid|fulvic acid]], giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref><ref name=":0">[https://www.aprotraveljapan.com/post/hokkaido-heritage-moor-onsen Hokkaido Heritage 'Moor Onsen']</ref><ref>https://www.hokkaidohotel.co.jp/english/stay/hotspring.html</ref><ref>https://www.daiichihotel.com/eng/spa/</ref><ref>https://att-japan.net/en/446-2/</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:wikt:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /><ref>http://www.tokachigawa.net/english/destination/tokachigawa.html</ref> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Germany|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ドイツ|1=Germany|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q183}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō Region]].<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, [[:en:Brine spring|Brine spring]], or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and [[:en:Russia|Russia]], finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * [[:en:Baden-Baden|Baden-Baden]]<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|qq=}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}} - Designated as a [[:en:Hokkaido Heritage|Hokkaido Heritage]].<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * [[:en:Yawatahama|Yawatahama]], Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:wikt:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /><ref>http://www.tokachigawa.net/english/destination/tokachigawa.html</ref> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Germany|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ドイツ|1=Germany|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q183}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō Region]].<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, [[:en:Brine spring|Brine spring]], or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and [[:en:Russia|Russia]], finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * [[:en:Baden-Baden|Baden-Baden]]<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|qq=}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}} - Designated as a [[:en:Hokkaido Heritage|Hokkaido Heritage]].<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * [[:en:Yawatahama|Yawatahama]], Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kuroyu|ja|黒湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroyu|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:モール泉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11344950]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}}, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Sub-bituminous_coal|Sub-bituminous coal]]]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing [[:en:Humic_substance|humic substances]] like humic acid and [[:en:Fulvic_acid|fulvic acid]], giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref><ref name=":0">[https://www.aprotraveljapan.com/post/hokkaido-heritage-moor-onsen Hokkaido Heritage 'Moor Onsen']</ref><ref>https://www.hokkaidohotel.co.jp/english/stay/hotspring.html</ref><ref>https://www.daiichihotel.com/eng/spa/</ref><ref>https://att-japan.net/en/446-2/</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:wikt:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /><ref>http://www.tokachigawa.net/english/destination/tokachigawa.html</ref> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Germany|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ドイツ|1=Germany|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q183}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō Region]].<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, [[:en:Brine spring|Brine spring]], or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and [[:en:Russia|Russia]], finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * [[:en:Baden-Baden|Baden-Baden]]<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|qq=}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}} - Designated as a [[:en:Hokkaido Heritage|Hokkaido Heritage]].<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * [[:en:Yawatahama|Yawatahama]], Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, [[:en:Brine spring|Brine spring]], or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and [[:en:Russia|Russia]], finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * [[:en:Baden-Baden|Baden-Baden]]<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|qq=}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}} - Designated as a [[:en:Hokkaido Heritage|Hokkaido Heritage]].<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * [[:en:Yawatahama|Yawatahama]], Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kuroyu|ja|黒湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroyu|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:モール泉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11344950]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}}, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Sub-bituminous_coal|Sub-bituminous coal]]]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing [[:en:Humic_substance|humic substances]] like humic acid and [[:en:Fulvic_acid|fulvic acid]], giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref><ref name=":0">[https://www.aprotraveljapan.com/post/hokkaido-heritage-moor-onsen Hokkaido Heritage 'Moor Onsen']</ref><ref>https://www.hokkaidohotel.co.jp/english/stay/hotspring.html</ref><ref>https://www.daiichihotel.com/eng/spa/</ref><ref>https://att-japan.net/en/446-2/</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:wikt:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /><ref>http://www.tokachigawa.net/english/destination/tokachigawa.html</ref> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Germany|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ドイツ|1=Germany|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q183}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō Region]].<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, [[:en:Brine spring|Brine spring]], or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and [[:en:Russia|Russia]], finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * [[:en:Baden-Baden|Baden-Baden]]<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|qq=}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}} - Designated as a [[:en:Hokkaido Heritage|Hokkaido Heritage]].<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * [[:en:Yawatahama|Yawatahama]], Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kuroyu|ja|黒湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroyu|qq=}}

The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, [[:en:Brine spring|Brine spring]], or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:モール泉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11344950]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}}, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Sub-bituminous_coal|Sub-bituminous coal]]]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing [[:en:Humic_substance|humic substances]] like humic acid and [[:en:Fulvic_acid|fulvic acid]], giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref><ref name=":0">[https://www.aprotraveljapan.com/post/hokkaido-heritage-moor-onsen Hokkaido Heritage 'Moor Onsen']</ref><ref>https://www.hokkaidohotel.co.jp/english/stay/hotspring.html</ref><ref>https://www.daiichihotel.com/eng/spa/</ref><ref>https://att-japan.net/en/446-2/</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:wikt:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /><ref>http://www.tokachigawa.net/english/destination/tokachigawa.html</ref> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Germany|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ドイツ|1=Germany|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q183}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō Region]].<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, [[:en:Brine spring|Brine spring]], or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and [[:en:Russia|Russia]], finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * [[:en:Baden-Baden|Baden-Baden]]<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|qq=}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}} - Designated as a [[:en:Hokkaido Heritage|Hokkaido Heritage]].<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * [[:en:Yawatahama|Yawatahama]], Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kuroyu|ja|黒湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroyu|qq=}}

Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Germany|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ドイツ|1=Germany|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q183}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />

The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, [[:en:Brine spring|Brine spring]], or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and [[:en:Russia|Russia]], finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:モール泉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11344950]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}}, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Sub-bituminous_coal|Sub-bituminous coal]]]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing [[:en:Humic_substance|humic substances]] like humic acid and [[:en:Fulvic_acid|fulvic acid]], giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref><ref name=":0">[https://www.aprotraveljapan.com/post/hokkaido-heritage-moor-onsen Hokkaido Heritage 'Moor Onsen']</ref><ref>https://www.hokkaidohotel.co.jp/english/stay/hotspring.html</ref><ref>https://www.daiichihotel.com/eng/spa/</ref><ref>https://att-japan.net/en/446-2/</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:wikt:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /><ref>http://www.tokachigawa.net/english/destination/tokachigawa.html</ref> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Germany|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ドイツ|1=Germany|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q183}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō Region]].<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, [[:en:Brine spring|Brine spring]], or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and [[:en:Russia|Russia]], finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * [[:en:Baden-Baden|Baden-Baden]]<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|qq=}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}} - Designated as a [[:en:Hokkaido Heritage|Hokkaido Heritage]].<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * [[:en:Yawatahama|Yawatahama]], Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kuroyu|ja|黒湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroyu|qq=}}

== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:wikt:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /><ref>http://www.tokachigawa.net/english/destination/tokachigawa.html</ref> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Germany|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ドイツ|1=Germany|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q183}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō Region]].<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, [[:en:Brine spring|Brine spring]], or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and [[:en:Russia|Russia]], finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * [[:en:Baden-Baden|Baden-Baden]]<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|qq=}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}} - Designated as a [[:en:Hokkaido Heritage|Hokkaido Heritage]].<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * [[:en:Yawatahama|Yawatahama]], Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Germany|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ドイツ|1=Germany|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q183}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō Region]].<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, [[:en:Brine spring|Brine spring]], or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and [[:en:Russia|Russia]], finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * [[:en:Baden-Baden|Baden-Baden]]<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|qq=}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}} - Designated as a [[:en:Hokkaido Heritage|Hokkaido Heritage]].<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * [[:en:Yawatahama|Yawatahama]], Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:wikt:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /><ref>http://www.tokachigawa.net/english/destination/tokachigawa.html</ref> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Germany|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ドイツ|1=Germany|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q183}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō Region]].<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, [[:en:Brine spring|Brine spring]], or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and [[:en:Russia|Russia]], finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />

== Representative Moor Springs == * [[:en:Baden-Baden|Baden-Baden]]<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|qq=}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}} - Designated as a [[:en:Hokkaido Heritage|Hokkaido Heritage]].<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * [[:en:Yawatahama|Yawatahama]], Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}}, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Sub-bituminous_coal|Sub-bituminous coal]]]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing [[:en:Humic_substance|humic substances]] like humic acid and [[:en:Fulvic_acid|fulvic acid]], giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref><ref name=":0">[https://www.aprotraveljapan.com/post/hokkaido-heritage-moor-onsen Hokkaido Heritage 'Moor Onsen']</ref><ref>https://www.hokkaidohotel.co.jp/english/stay/hotspring.html</ref><ref>https://www.daiichihotel.com/eng/spa/</ref><ref>https://att-japan.net/en/446-2/</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:wikt:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /><ref>http://www.tokachigawa.net/english/destination/tokachigawa.html</ref> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Germany|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ドイツ|1=Germany|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q183}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō Region]].<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, [[:en:Brine spring|Brine spring]], or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and [[:en:Russia|Russia]], finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * [[:en:Baden-Baden|Baden-Baden]]<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|qq=}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}} - Designated as a [[:en:Hokkaido Heritage|Hokkaido Heritage]].<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * [[:en:Yawatahama|Yawatahama]], Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture

The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, [[:en:Brine spring|Brine spring]], or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan11s.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}}, a moor hot spring]] [[File:131012 Tokachigawa Onsen Otofuke Hokkaido Japan10s.jpg|thumb|250px|[[:en:Sub-bituminous_coal|Sub-bituminous coal]]]] '''Moor Springs''' (モール泉, Mōru-sen) refer to hot springs that flow through peat layers, containing [[:en:Humic_substance|humic substances]] like humic acid and [[:en:Fulvic_acid|fulvic acid]], giving them a transparent, brownish hue.<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |author1=Genki Inoue |author2=Risako Shimizu |author3=Mika Nagura |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Black Hot Springs in Ōta Ward, Tokyo |journal=Proceedings of the Japan Geochemical Society Annual Meeting |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref><ref name="isan">{{Cite web |url=https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/pdf/isan_06.pdf |title=Chapter 2 of "Town, People, Hokkaido Heritage" Vol. 6 |publisher=Hokkaido Heritage Promotion Council |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref><ref name=":0">[https://www.aprotraveljapan.com/post/hokkaido-heritage-moor-onsen Hokkaido Heritage 'Moor Onsen']</ref><ref>https://www.hokkaidohotel.co.jp/english/stay/hotspring.html</ref><ref>https://www.daiichihotel.com/eng/spa/</ref><ref>https://att-japan.net/en/446-2/</ref> They are also known as "moor hot springs" or "moor-type hot springs."<ref name="takano2019">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Humic Substances and Visual Indicators of Moor-Type Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Onsen Science |volume=68 |year=2019 |pages=240-251 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol68_pdf/vol68no4_240_251.pdf}}</ref>
== Overview ==
The term "moor" derives from the German word "[[:wikt:Moor|Moor]]," meaning peat.<ref name="isan" /><ref>http://www.tokachigawa.net/english/destination/tokachigawa.html</ref> Non-volcanic hot springs originating in [[:en:Alluvial_plains|alluvial plains]] often pass through [[:en:Peat|peat]] layers, resulting in springs with more plant-derived components than mineral ones. These springs are characterized by brown water due to humic substances like humic acid and fulvic acid.<ref name="isan" /><ref name="takano2019" />
Representative moor springs include Baden-Baden in {{ill|Germany|ja|ドイツ連邦共和国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Germany|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ドイツ|1=Germany|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q183}} and Tokachigawa Onsen in [[:en:Otofuke,_Hokkaido|Otofuke]], [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], as well as locations in the Ishikari Plain and Toyotomi.<ref name="isan" /><ref name=":0" />
Hot springs tinted brown by dissolved organic matter (DOM), including humic acid and fulvic acid, are referred to as "brown hot springs."<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Eiji Uchino |author3=Naoki Aoyagi |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Content in Humic Substances from Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Limnology and Oceanography |volume=79 |issue=3 |year=2018 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref> "Moor-type hot springs" and "black water" are regional names; for instance, they are called "moor-type hot springs" in Hokkaido and "black water" in [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō Region]].<ref name="takano2018" /> Moreover, comparative studies have found differences in C/N ratios and C/P ratios between black water in Kanagawa Prefecture and moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido.<ref>{{Cite journal |author1=Takashi Takano |author2=Genki Inoue |author3=Eiji Uchino |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Black Water Hot Springs in Kanagawa Prefecture |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>
The Japanese {{ill|Onsen Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}}'s definition of therapeutic springs does not include categories related to humic substances, but the [[:en:Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] guidelines for hot spring analysis include humic acid as an analyte.<ref name="takano2019" /> Within the classification of therapeutic springs, moor springs may be categorized as {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, [[:en:Brine spring|Brine spring]], or {{ill|Bicarbonate Springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|1=Bicarbonate springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}}, depending on their specific components.
In Nordic countries and [[:en:Russia|Russia]], finely powdered peat is sometimes used for bathing.<ref name="isan" />
== Representative Moor Springs == * [[:en:Baden-Baden|Baden-Baden]]<ref name="isan" /> * {{ill|Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|ja|西那須野温泉#杜の湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinasuno Onsen#Mori-no-Yu|qq=}} (Mori-no-Yu) * {{ill|Tokachigawa Onsen|ja|十勝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokachigawa Onsen|qq=}} - Designated as a [[:en:Hokkaido Heritage|Hokkaido Heritage]].<ref>[https://www.hokkaidoisan.org/heritage_033.html Moor Hot Springs] NPO Hokkaido Heritage Secretariat</ref><ref name=":0" /> * [[:en:Yawatahama|Yawatahama]], Ehime Prefecture - The first moor spring in the Chūgoku and Shikoku regions.<ref>[http://www.yawa-minatoyu.jp/index.html Yawatahama Black Hot Spring Minato-yu]</ref> * Ringo Onsen - Asahi Town, Nishimurayama District, Yamagata Prefecture
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kuroyu|ja|黒湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroyu|qq=}}
== References ==
